content.full_text:
James F. Wilburn
Certified Parliamentarian
7159 N. Fruit Ave.
Fresno, CA 93711-0740
May 31, 1990

Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J STREET
SACRAMENTO, CA. 95814

Dear Sirs:

The Fresno City Council Resolution Number 81-462 created a
Citizen Advisory Commission. Pursuant to the ordinance, the
Citizen Advisory Commission is to promote full and open
discussion, provide balanced and objective information, and
provide a forum for generating ideas and alternatives of issues
to advise the Fresno City Council. The Commission's bylaws,
Article IX name the latest edition of Robert's Rules of Order as
it parliamentary authority.

Enclosed are two letters to Mr. Chris Petersen, May 1, 1990.
Councilman Petersen inquired of Mr. Robert W. Leiman, President
of American Institute of Parliamentarians, about published
minutes. Mr. Leiman referred the matter to Mr. James Wilburn who
took up the matter with the Fresno Chapter of American Institute
of Parliamentarians. The findings were forwarded to Mr.
Petersen. Mr. Wilburn also pointed out areas of danger he noted
in the bylaws and actions of the Commission.

Mr. Wilburn sent a letter to the Council concerning a
specific Commission meeting. On May 21, 1990 the chairman of the
Commission did not aid nor allow a member to amend a motion. The
chairman closed debate and put the pending question to a vote
without properly putting the previous question. The chairman did
not allow the member his rights as a parliamentarian of the
Citizen Advisory Commission participated in the proceedings,
showing partiality.

I ask for an opinion on the matters contained in the
letters. Are the premises correct? Were there abuses of civil
rights? Did the Commission act pursuant to its duty? If there
are abuses of civil rights, the abuses need correction. Where
misinformation results in abuses, mend the abuses with correct
information.

Sincerely,

James F. Wilburn
James F. Wilburn

James F. Wilburn
7159 N. Fruit Ave.
Fresno, CA 93711-0740
May 1, 1990


Hon. Chris Peterson, Council Member
City of Fresno
2326 Fresno Street
Fresno, CA 93721

Dear Council-member Peterson:

     Under separate cover you will find an opinion of the
question Mr. Clyde Jones raised about published minutes. You
said Mr. Jones is often correct. The opinion of the Fresno
Chapter American Institute of Parliamentarians is that he is
correct again. I have had conversations with Mr. Jones on other
subjects. I attended a meeting of the Citizens Advisory
Commission with him. I have are other concerns. This letter
will address three.

     In the transcript of a portion of the April 1990 meeting of
the Citizens Advisor Commission, the chairman said, "You called
for the question, is that what you're asking for? All those if
favor of the motion, signify by saying aye. (Only two voices
responded with Aye . . . Jones and Lambert) Oppos? (No
response) Motion carries." It sounds as though the chairman
assumed a motion of previous question without a vote. Previous
question stops debate and requires a two-thirds vote, it should
be counted. (Robert's Rules of Order Newly Revised, 1990 ed., pp.
194-207) Once debate is ended, the motion is then put to a
vote. The transcript indicates only the motion was put to a
vote, skipping a vote on previous question.

     The misuse of the motion is one concern, the other is
whether it should be allowed in Commission meetings. If the
purpose of the commission is as a board or committee where
unlimited discussion is desired in order to have greater input,
the previous question motions are inadvisable. Small group
dynamics suggest consensus as a goal. By consensus is meant the
group works toward a general agreement to which it is committed.

     The third concern is Article IV, Section (2), "The
chairperson shall have the authority to establish Ad Hoc
Committee, upon a need established by the Commission, at which
time the express purpose of the Committee shall be narrowly
defined." This section is ambiguous. Too often, a person will
read up to the first comma then act. The reminder of the
requirement of approval by the Commission is forgotten. Such a
committee can be created and act without authority.

     Mr. Jones said in the meeting of Fresno Chapter American
Institute of Parliamentarians April 24, 1990 that some people
call him a gadfly. If he is a gadfly, he is in good company.
Without the gadfly, the Unites States would not exist. Mr. Jones

1

may be abrasive to some, but we need his ideas. If there is
conflict, let us recognize the conflict and deal with it. Better
knowledge of small group dynamics and rules can help any
commission, it can help in Fresno. My intent is that democratic
principles be practiced and preserved.

Sincerely,

James F. Wilburn
James F. Wilburn,
Certified Parliamentarian
Candidate for Master of Arts
Degree, California State
University, Fresno.

Copy: Clyde Jones

2

James F. Wilburn
Certified Parliamentarian
7159 N. Fruit Ave.
Fresno, CA 93744-4946
May 28, 1990

Hon. Karen Humphrey
2326 FRESNO STREET
FRESNO, CA 93721

Dear Hon. Karen Humphrey:

It is crushing to think that ignorance of established rules ruins
the hard work of a volunteer group. It is crushing to experience
personal loss due to one's own ignorance, but it is greater anguish
when one's ignorance brings about abuse of the right of others. It is
crushing to realize an intended good deed results in injustice to
others. The group about which I write this paper worked hard on a
difficult task, yet there were abuses.

This paper explains how individual and public rights were abused in
the Fresno City Citizen Advisory Commission May 21, 1990. This paper
shows how misuse of rules contributed to abuse of rights. It suggests
solutions for courtesy and effectiveness in decision-making. The focus
is on three examples. There are no names because the concern is for
principles, not personalities.

The examples deal with closing debate, information to members, and
impartiality of the presiding officer and parliamentarian of the
Citizens Advisory Commission. Members of deliberative bodies have the
right to discuss, amend, and vote on any pending business. Members
also have a right to be well informed of their rights particularly when
the member requests information. Members control the meetings, the
presiding officer is a facilitator. The parliamentarian is an advisor.

Robert's Rules of Order Newly Revised, Ninth Edition, 1990, p. 199
emphasizes in italics that call for the question requires a second and
two-thirds vote to stop debate. The presiding officer of the Citizens
Advisory Commission stopped debate repeatedly one member called for the
question. The specific example in which an individual's rights were
abused was at 8:14 pm when a member asked if he could amend the pending
motion. The presiding officer's response was, the chairman recognized
a member who was not the one seeking amendment information and noted
this second member had called for the question. The chairman put vote
immediately without putting the previous question to a vote.

The first two abuses took place in this one action. There was not
vote on the call for the question. The member received no information
to his inquiry to amend the motion that was pending before the

¹ Sarah Corbin Robert, Assisted by Henry M. Robert and William J.
Evans. The Scott, Foresman Robert's Rules of Order
Newly Revised, 9th ed., 1990. Glenview, Illinois: Scott,
Foresman, and Company. p. 199. (RONR, 1990)

1

assembly.' The chairman did not allow the assembly to express its
opinion correctly.' Not only the commissioner's rights were abused, the
public he represents also suffers the same abuse.

The impartiality of the parliamentarian is the third abuse of rights
of the Citizens Advisory Commission.' I showed this reference to the
presiding officer at 8:50 pm during a recess in the meeting. The
parliamentarian continued to make motions, debate, and vote. The
parliamentarian did not call to the attention of the chair proceedings
that substantively affected the rights of members and the public.'

The first solution is to provide information and instructions to
commissioners in meeting procedures. For a second suggestion, avoid
call for the question. A rule or policy facilitates this restriction.
The third solution is to provide an impartial parliamentarian familiar
with group dynamics and rules of meetings as an advisor to commissions.'
Knowledge of group dynamics provides variety in decision-making
procedures.'

These are examples that definitely had an effect on the decision of
the Citizens Advisory Commission. Had there been guidance instead of
direction, there would be a different decision. Consensus decision is
better than a vote decision at times.'

                                                    Sincerely,

                                                    James F. Wilburn
                                                    James F. Wilburn

Note: The information in this letter goes to Fresno City
Councilmembers, Mayor, Manager, Clerk, and Attorney; Sacramento, the
Fair Political Practices Commission; Fresno American Civil Liberties
Union; the Fresno Daily Report and the Fresno Bee.


_______________________

²  RONR, 1990, p. 441.

³  RONR, 1990, p. 227.

⁴  RONR, 1990, p. 458

⁵  RONR, 1990, p. 457.

⁶  RONR, 1990, p. 456.

⁷  John W. Ray, "Parliamentary Procedure and Democratic Theory: Two
Perspectives," Parliamentary Journal, April, 1990, 31, #2, pp. 63-69.

⁸  RONR, 1990.

                                                    2
